cards.title: Geriatric Assessment — BNYS Approach
cards._legacyHtml:
Geriatric Giants — 5 Is
Immobility · Instability (falls) · Incontinence · Intellectual impairment (dementia/delirium) · Iatrogenic disease (polypharmacy). Address these systematically in every elderly naturopathic patient.
Assessment ToolWhat It TestsThresholdAction if Abnormal
MMSE / MoCACognitive functionMMSE <24, MoCA <26 = impairedRefer geriatrics/neurology
GDS-15Geriatric depression scaleScore >5 = likely depressionCounselling, omega-3, Vit D, refer if severe
Timed Up & Go (TUG)Falls risk / mobility>12 seconds = high falls riskStrength training, balance exercises, home assessment
Grip strengthSarcopenia / frailty<26kg men, <16kg women = lowProtein 1.2–1.5g/kg/day, resistance training, Vit D
Nutritional screening (MNA)Malnutrition riskScore <17 = malnutritionProtein supplementation, dietitian referral
Medication reviewPolypharmacy / Beers criteria>5 drugs = polypharmacyReview with prescribing physician — deprescribing

cards.title: Sarcopenia & Frailty — Naturopathic Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
Sarcopenia Definition
Progressive loss of skeletal muscle mass, strength and function with age. After 50: lose 1–2% muscle mass/year. After 70: lose up to 3%/year. Associated with falls, fractures, hospitalisation, and mortality.
InterventionEvidenceProtocol
Protein intake ★★★★★ Strong1.2–1.5g/kg/day (RDA of 0.8g is insufficient for elderly). Distribute: 30–40g/meal. Leucine-rich sources: eggs, whey, fish, legumes.
Resistance training ★★★★★ Strong2–3x/week, all major muscle groups, progressive overload. Even chair-based exercises in frail elderly increase muscle mass.
Vitamin D3★★★★ Strong2000–4000 IU/day. Deficiency causes myopathy and proximal weakness. Reduces falls risk 20% in deficient elderly.
Creatine monohydrate★★★ Good3–5g/day with resistance training. Increases muscle mass and strength in elderly. Safe long-term.
Omega-3★★★ Good2–3g EPA+DHA/day. Anti-inflammatory, anabolic signalling in muscle.
HMB (β-hydroxy-β-methylbutyrate)★★★ Good3g/day. Reduces muscle protein breakdown. Particularly useful in bed-bound/hospitalised elderly.
🟡 Anabolic Resistance
Elderly muscle is "anabolically resistant" — it requires MORE protein stimulus to achieve same muscle protein synthesis as young adults. Key: (1) Higher leucine intake (3g/meal minimum) — leucine is the anabolic trigger. (2) Protein at breakfast (most elderly eat minimal morning protein). (3) Exercise BEFORE protein (maximises anabolic window). (4) Creatine + protein + resistance training = strongest combination for sarcopenia reversal in trials.

cards.title: Polypharmacy & Beers Criteria — BNYS Reference
cards._legacyHtml:
Polypharmacy = ≥5 medications
70% of elderly take ≥1 medication; 36% take ≥5. Adverse drug reactions are the 4th leading cause of death in elderly. The Beers Criteria identifies medications that are potentially inappropriate in adults ≥65.
Drug CategorySpecific DrugsRisk in ElderlyNaturopathic Alternative
Benzodiazepines ★Diazepam, lorazepam, temazepamFalls, fractures, cognitive impairment, dependenceValerian, L-theanine, CBT-I, Mg glycinate, melatonin
Anticholinergics ★Diphenhydramine, amitriptyline, oxybutyninConfusion, delirium, constipation, urinary retention, fallsAddress underlying cause — not mask with anticholinergics
NSAIDs (long-term) ★Ibuprofen, naproxen, diclofenacGI bleeding, renal failure, fluid retention, CVDTurmeric, fish oil, Boswellia, physiotherapy, hydrotherapy
HypnoticsZopiclone, zolpidemFalls, fractures, dependence, rebound insomniaSleep hygiene, melatonin, valerian, CBT-I
PPIs (long-term)Omeprazole, pantoprazoleB12, Mg deficiency, fractures, infectionsDGL, dietary modification, deprescribing protocol
🟡 Deprescribing Pearl
Never stop medications abruptly without physician involvement — but you CAN initiate the conversation. Approach: "I notice you're taking X medication, which can sometimes cause Y symptom you're experiencing. It might be worth discussing with your doctor whether this medication is still necessary." Arm the patient with information. The STOPP/START tool (used by physicians) identifies 80+ potentially inappropriate prescribing patterns in elderly. Refer patients to their GP with a polypharmacy review request if ≥5 medications.

cards.title: Falls Prevention — Comprehensive Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
Falls — Leading Cause of Injury Death in Elderly
1 in 3 adults over 65 falls each year. Hip fracture 1-year mortality: 20–30%. Multifactorial intervention reduces falls by 30–40%.
1
Risk Assessment Timed Up and Go (TUG): rise from chair, walk 3m, turn, return, sit. Normal <12 sec. High risk >20 sec. Also assess: vision, postural BP (drop >20mmHg = orthostatic hypotension), feet and footwear, home hazards, medications (Beers list).
2
Exercise — Most Important Intervention Otago Exercise Programme (balance + leg strength 3x/week) reduces falls 35%. Tai Chi reduces falls 43%. Focus on: single-leg standing 10 sec, heel-toe walking, sit-to-stand (strengthens quads — most important fall-prevention muscle). Prescribe structured programme with physiotherapist.
3
Vitamin D Deficiency impairs muscle strength and balance. Target 25(OH)D >75 nmol/L. Dose: 2000–4000 IU/day + Calcium 1200mg + K2 100mcg. Vit D reduces falls by 20% in deficient elderly patients.
4
Medication Review High-risk medications: benzodiazepines (2x falls risk), antihypertensives (orthostatic hypotension), antipsychotics (sedation), opioids, diuretics (nocturia, orthostasis). Deprescribing benzodiazepines alone reduces falls 40%. Work with prescribing physician.
5
Home Safety Remove loose rugs, clutter, poor lighting. Add grab rails (bathroom, toilet, stairs), non-slip bath mat, raised toilet seat, nightlights. Footwear: well-fitting, low-heeled, non-slip — avoid loose slippers.

cards.title: Cognitive Decline — Early Detection & Prevention
cards._legacyHtml:
StageFeaturesMoCANaturopathic Approach
Normal ageingSlower processing, occasional word-finding, ADLs intact26–30Mediterranean/MIND diet, exercise, sleep, social engagement — preventive
Mild Cognitive ImpairmentNoticeable decline, ADLs preserved, patient/family worried19–25MIND diet + aerobic exercise 150min/week + sleep 7–8hrs + Vit D + omega-3 + Lion's Mane + cognitive training
Mild dementiaADL impairment, getting lost, repeating questions, personality change10–18Maintain routine, safe environment, caregiver support, omega-3, Vit E 2000IU (mild evidence), music therapy, exercise
Moderate-severe dementiaDependent ADLs, incontinence, behavioural symptoms<10Comfort, dignity, caregiver support — refer geriatrics/memory clinic
🟡 Key Modifiable Risk Factors (Lancet 2020)
The 12 modifiable dementia risk factors and their population-attributable risk: low education (7%), hearing loss (8%), hypertension (2%), obesity (1%), depression (4%), physical inactivity (2%), diabetes (1%), smoking (5%), alcohol (1%), social isolation (4%), air pollution (2%), head injury (3%) = 40% of all dementia potentially preventable. Address in this order of impact: hearing loss, depression, social isolation, smoking, physical inactivity. Simple, accessible, free — align with naturopathic philosophy.

cards.title: Urinary Incontinence — Naturopathic Assessment & Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
Urinary Incontinence — Underreported
UI affects 30–40% of community-dwelling elderly and up to 70% of nursing home residents. Most patients are too embarrassed to report it — always ask directly. It is treatable — not an inevitable part of ageing.
TypeMechanismFeaturesNaturopathic Approach
Stress incontinenceWeak pelvic floor — urethral sphincter incompetenceLeakage with coughing, sneezing, laughing, exercisePelvic floor exercises (Kegel) ★★★★, pessary for women, weight loss, avoid caffeine
Urge incontinence (OAB)Detrusor overactivity — involuntary bladder contractionSudden urge, cannot reach toilet in time, frequency >8x/day, nocturiaBladder training ★★★★, avoid caffeine/alcohol, pumpkin seed extract, magnesium, acupuncture
Mixed (most common in elderly)Both stress + urge componentsBoth leakage typesCombination pelvic floor + bladder training. Address both components
Overflow incontinenceBladder outlet obstruction (BPH men) or underactive detrusorDribbling, incomplete emptying, weak stream, distended bladderRefer — may need catheterisation. Saw palmetto for BPH adjunct
Functional incontinenceCannot reach toilet due to mobility/cognitive issuesNormal bladder but cannot toilet independentlyPrompted voiding, toilet accessibility (grab rails, commode), scheduled toileting
K
Kegel (Pelvic Floor) Exercises Identify correct muscles: stop urinary stream mid-flow (identification only — not regular exercise). Contract pelvic floor, hold 3–5 sec, relax 3–5 sec. Progress to 10 sec hold. 3 sets of 10–15 reps, 3x/day. Results in 6–12 weeks. Biofeedback-assisted pelvic floor training more effective than verbal instruction alone. Refer to pelvic floor physiotherapist for optimal results.
B
Bladder Training (for Urge UI) Scheduled voiding every 2 hours regardless of urge. When urge occurs: STOP, breathe, squeeze pelvic floor × 5, walk calmly to toilet (do not rush — rushing increases urgency). Gradually extend voiding interval by 15 min every week until 3–4 hourly. 6–8 week programme — success rate 75%+. As effective as antimuscarinic medications with no side effects.
D
Dietary Modifications Eliminate: caffeine (irritates detrusor — even decaf), alcohol, artificial sweeteners, carbonated drinks, spicy foods (all bladder irritants). Target 1.5–2L plain water/day — neither restrict (concentrates urine → irritation) nor excess. Pumpkin seed extract 500mg BD — Grade B evidence for urge and stress UI. Magnesium 400mg/day — reduces detrusor overactivity.

cards.title: Pain Management in Elderly — Non-Pharmacological Protocols
cards._legacyHtml:
Why Non-Pharmacological Pain Management is Critical in Elderly
NSAIDs in elderly: GI bleeding risk 3x (age >65), renal impairment, cardiovascular risk, fluid retention. Opioids: falls risk, constipation, cognitive impairment, dependence. Non-pharmacological approaches should be FIRST-LINE in elderly chronic pain.
InterventionEvidenceBest ForProtocol
Exercise (supervised)★★★★ Strongest for chronic painOA, LBP, fibromyalgia, neuropathicLow-impact: water aerobics, walking, tai chi. 30 min 5x/week. Start slow, progress weekly
TENS★★★ GoodLocalised pain, OA, LBP, post-herpetic neuralgia20–30 min session, 1–2x/day. Home units available. Safe in elderly — avoid pacemaker
Heat therapy★★★ GoodMuscle pain, arthritis (chronic), back painHot pack 15–20 min. Wax bath for hands. Warm bath/shower AM for morning stiffness
Cognitive Behavioural Therapy for Pain★★★★ StrongChronic pain with catastrophising, depression overlay8-week structured programme. Reduces pain scores AND disability. Pain Catastrophising Scale (PCS) to identify candidates
Acupressure/Acupuncture★★★ GoodOA (knee, hip), LBP, headacheAcupressure: ST36, SP6, LI4, BL60. 6–10 sessions for OA. Cochrane confirms modest but real benefit
Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction★★★★ StrongFibromyalgia, chronic widespread pain8-week programme. Reduces pain intensity + pain-related disability. Retrains pain neuromatrix
Topical applications★★★ GoodLocalised OA, neuropathic painCapsaicin 0.075% QID × 4–6 weeks (depletes substance P). Comfrey root gel. Arnica gel. Topical diclofenac (prescriptive). All avoid systemic side effects
🟡 Pain Assessment in Dementia
Patients with moderate-severe dementia cannot reliably self-report pain using NRS/VAS. Use: PAINAD (Pain Assessment in Advanced Dementia): observes breathing, negative vocalisation, facial expression, body language, consolability. Score 0–10. Treat proactively — undertreated pain in dementia causes aggression, agitation, and wandering that is often misattributed to dementia behaviour rather than unrecognised pain. Regular scheduled analgesia (not PRN only) is recommended for known painful conditions in dementia.

cards.title: Palliative & End-of-Life Care — Naturopathic Role
cards._legacyHtml:
Palliative Care — The Naturopathic Philosophy Applies Most Here
Palliative naturopathy focuses on: dignity, comfort, quality of life, and whole-person care. The naturopathic principles of "first do no harm" and "treat the whole person" are never more relevant than at end of life. Goals shift from cure to comfort and meaning.
SymptomNaturopathic InterventionEvidence
PainMassage, TENS, heat, topical capsaicin, acupressure, relaxation techniques. Adjunct to opioids — do not replace★★★ Good as adjuncts
Nausea/vomitingGinger 1g TDS, PC6 acupressure, peppermint aromatherapy, small cold meals★★★ Good
Fatigue (cancer-related)Gentle walking 20–30 min/day (strongest evidence), rest-activity balance, gentle yoga, adaptogens (Ashwagandha — caution in advanced cancer — check oncologist)★★★★ Exercise is gold standard
Anxiety/existential distressMindfulness, guided imagery, music therapy, breathwork, spiritual care, therapeutic presence★★★★ MBSR in cancer — strong
InsomniaSleep hygiene, melatonin 5–10mg, valerian, relaxation, lavender aromatherapy, weighted blanket★★★ Good
Anorexia/cachexiaSmall frequent high-calorie meals, favourite foods (quality over quantity), oral nutritional supplements, mouth care, addressing constipation, ginger for nausea★★★ Good
Constipation (opioid-induced)Senna + docusate (standard), Mg oxide, warm prune juice, abdominal massage, methylnaltrexone (prescriptive — PAMORA)★★★★ Essential — all opioid patients need laxatives
🟡 The Naturopathic Role in Dying
The most profound naturopathic contribution in palliative care is often NOT the supplement or herb — it is: therapeutic presence (being fully present with suffering), facilitating meaning-making (what has mattered in this life?), supporting the family system (caregiver burden is a clinical condition), and ensuring death with dignity. Integrate with the palliative care team — share information, respect the plan, and do not introduce interventions that create false hope or add burden. Vis medicatrix naturae: the innate healing power includes the capacity to die with grace.

cards.title: Dementia — Practical Management Guide
cards._legacyHtml:
Dementia in India — 8.8 Million Affected
Alzheimer's (60–70%), vascular (15–20%), Lewy body (5–10%), FTD (5%). BNYS practitioners often first contact for early cognitive symptoms.
TypeKey Distinguishing FeaturesBNYS Role
Alzheimer'sInsidious onset, episodic memory first, progressive. ApoE4 genetic risk.Prevention (ReCODE), lifestyle intervention, caregiver education
Vascular dementiaStepwise progression, post-stroke, frontal/executive impairment, gait disturbanceCV risk factor management, Mediterranean diet, exercise
Lewy body (LBD)Visual hallucinations, fluctuating cognition, Parkinsonism, RBD. EXTREME sensitivity to antipsychotics (can be fatal).Refer neurology. Educate carers on neuroleptic danger.
FrontotemporalPersonality change, disinhibition, apathy. Younger onset (50–65). Preserved memory early.Caregiver support, refer neuropsychiatry
1
Non-Pharmacological ManagementCognitive stimulation therapy (CST) ★★★ — equivalent to acetylcholinesterase inhibitors for cognition and QoL. Reality orientation therapy. Reminiscence therapy (photos/music from formative years). Music therapy ★★★ — reduces BPSD. Structured routine — reduces sundowning.
2
Caregiver SupportCaregiver burnout affects 60–70% — screen with PHQ-9 + GAD-7 at every visit. Provide: stage education, practical strategies (wandering, agitation, eating difficulties), respite care advocacy, support groups. Legal planning (power of attorney, advance directive) — discuss early while patient has capacity.

cards.title: Nutrition in Older Adults
cards._legacyHtml:
Malnutrition — Most Underdiagnosed Condition in Geriatrics
Affects 15–30% of older adults. MNA (Mini Nutritional Assessment) should be administered to every patient over 70 at first consultation.
ProblemPrevalenceNaturopathic Intervention
Anorexia of ageing15–30%Small frequent meals, zinc 15mg (taste restoration), ginger, bitter herbs, social eating, treat depression
Sarcopenia25% of over 70s, 50% of over 80sProtein 1.6–2.4g/kg (anabolic resistance = MORE protein needed). Creatine 5g/day ★★★ + resistance training ★★★. Vit D 3000–5000 IU ★★★.
Micronutrient deficienciesB12 30%, Vit D 70%+B12 methylcobalamin 1000mcg (sublingual or IM if atrophic gastritis). Vit D 3000–5000 IU. Zinc 15mg. Mg 300mg. Check annually.
Dysphagia15% community, 50% nursing homeSLT referral — IDDSI texture modification. Upright 90° for meals. Small bites, slow eating. Urgent SLT if: coughing/choking or recurrent chest infections.
🟡 Protein Distribution — As Important as Total Protein
Each meal should contain 25–30g protein (leucine threshold 2.5–3g) to maximally stimulate MPS in elderly. Many eat minimal protein at breakfast. Add: eggs (6–7g each), paneer (25g/100g), Greek yogurt (15g/150g). Bedtime casein protein 40g maximises nocturnal MPS. Protein distribution strategy is as important as total daily intake in elderly sarcopenia.

cards.title: Sleep in Older Adults
cards._legacyHtml:
Age-Related Changes vs Pathological Sleep
Normal ageing: earlier sleep phase, more fragmented, reduced SWS — but NOT reduced sleep NEED. Total need remains 7–8 hours. "I don't need much sleep" is a common but inaccurate belief in elderly.
Age-Related ChangeNaturopathic Response
Advanced sleep phase syndromeEvening bright light 2500 lux (7–9pm). Delay dinner to 7pm. Gradual sleep delay protocol.
↓ Sleep spindles + SWSExercise ★★★ (increases SWS). Magnesium threonate 200mg (crosses BBB, increases SWS). Bedroom cool 18–20°C.
↓ Melatonin secretionMelatonin 0.5–1mg (NOT 5–10mg) 1–2hrs before sleep. Low dose more physiological in elderly.
Medication-induced insomniaReview: diuretics to morning, beta-blockers to morning. Beers Criteria review. CBT-I before hypnotics — more effective long-term.
1
REM Sleep Behaviour Disorder (RBD)Acting out vivid dreams during REM (punching, kicking, yelling). RBD is a prodromal marker for Parkinson's/Lewy body dementia — 80% develop synucleinopathy within 12 years. Refer neurology for polysomnography. Melatonin 3–12mg at bedtime ★★★ (first-line). Safety: pillow barrier, floor mattress for bed partner.
2
Sundowning ManagementBright light 10,000 lux from 2–4pm ★★★ (resets circadian rhythm, reduces frequency and severity). Melatonin 0.5–1mg at 6pm (BEFORE sundowning occurs — not 10pm). Structured afternoon activity. Minimise daytime napping. Caregiver education: distressing but not dangerous.

cards.title: Functional Independence — ADL Assessment
cards._legacyHtml:
ToolWhat It MeasuresScoring
Barthel ADL IndexBasic ADLs (feeding, bathing, grooming, dressing, bowel, bladder, toilet, transfers, mobility, stairs)0–100. <40 = severe dependence. 100 = independent. Min clinically significant change: 10 points.
Lawton IADL ScaleInstrumental ADLs (phone, shopping, food prep, housekeeping, laundry, transport, medications, finances)0–8. 8 = independent. Decline in IADLs before ADLs = early cognitive decline. Most sensitive to early change.
Timed Up and Go (TUG)Physical mobility, fall risk — rise from chair, walk 3m, return, sit<10 sec = normal. >20 sec = HIGH FALL RISK → urgent physiotherapy. >30 sec = dependent in mobility.
SPPB (Short Physical Performance Battery)Balance tests + 4m gait speed + 5 chair standsScore 0–12. ≤6 = high disability risk. Gait speed <0.8m/s alone = frailty marker.
Clock Drawing TestQuick cognitive screen (visuospatial + executive)Errors in placing numbers or hands = cognitive impairment concern. Use with MMSE or MoCA.
1
BNYS Geriatric Yoga ProtocolChair yoga: seated Tadasana, Gomukhasana, spinal twist, Virabhadrasana arms, forward bend. Standing with support: wall Tadasana, single-leg balance with chair. Evidence: yoga reduces falls by 50% in community-dwelling elderly (Cochrane 2020). Session: 30–45 min, 2–3×/week minimum.

Anorexia of ageing15–30%Small frequent meals, zinc 15mg (taste restoration), ginger, bitter herbs, social eating, treat depression
Sarcopenia25% of over 70s, 50% of over 80sProtein 1.6–2.4g/kg (anabolic resistance = MORE protein needed). Creatine 5g/day ★★★ + resistance training ★★★. Vit D 3000–5000 IU ★★★.
Micronutrient deficienciesB12 30%, Vit D 70%+B12 methylcobalamin 1000mcg (sublingual or IM if atrophic gastritis). Vit D 3000–5000 IU. Zinc 15mg. Mg 300mg. Check annually.
Dysphagia15% community, 50% nursing homeSLT referral — IDDSI texture modification. Upright 90° for meals. Small bites, slow eating. Urgent SLT if: coughing/choking or recurrent chest infections.
🟡 Protein Distribution — As Important as Total Protein
Each meal should contain 25–30g protein (leucine threshold 2.5–3g) to maximally stimulate MPS in elderly. Many eat minimal protein at breakfast. Add: eggs (6–7g each), paneer (25g/100g), Greek yogurt (15g/150g). Bedtime casein protein 40g maximises nocturnal MPS. Protein distribution strategy is as important as total daily intake in elderly sarcopenia.

cards.title: Cognitive Decline — Early Detection & Prevention
cards._legacyHtml:
StageFeaturesMoCANaturopathic Approach
Normal ageingSlower processing, occasional word-finding, ADLs intact26–30Mediterranean/MIND diet, exercise, sleep, social engagement — preventive
Mild Cognitive ImpairmentNoticeable decline, ADLs preserved, patient/family worried19–25MIND diet + aerobic exercise 150min/week + sleep 7–8hrs + Vit D + omega-3 + Lion's Mane + cognitive training
Mild dementiaADL impairment, getting lost, repeating questions, personality change10–18Maintain routine, safe environment, caregiver support, omega-3, Vit E 2000IU (mild evidence), music therapy, exercise
Moderate-severe dementiaDependent ADLs, incontinence, behavioural symptoms<10Comfort, dignity, caregiver support — refer geriatrics/memory clinic
🟡 Key Modifiable Risk Factors (Lancet 2020)
The 12 modifiable dementia risk factors and their population-attributable risk: low education (7%), hearing loss (8%), hypertension (2%), obesity (1%), depression (4%), physical inactivity (2%), diabetes (1%), smoking (5%), alcohol (1%), social isolation (4%), air pollution (2%), head injury (3%) = 40% of all dementia potentially preventable. Address in this order of impact: hearing loss, depression, social isolation, smoking, physical inactivity. Simple, accessible, free — align with naturopathic philosophy.

cards.title: Dementia — Practical Management Guide
cards._legacyHtml:
Dementia in India — 8.8 Million Affected
Alzheimer's (60–70%), vascular (15–20%), Lewy body (5–10%), FTD (5%). BNYS practitioners often first contact for early cognitive symptoms.
TypeKey Distinguishing FeaturesBNYS Role
Alzheimer'sInsidious onset, episodic memory first, progressive. ApoE4 genetic risk.Prevention (ReCODE), lifestyle intervention, caregiver education
Vascular dementiaStepwise progression, post-stroke, frontal/executive impairment, gait disturbanceCV risk factor management, Mediterranean diet, exercise
Lewy body (LBD)Visual hallucinations, fluctuating cognition, Parkinsonism, RBD. EXTREME sensitivity to antipsychotics (can be fatal).Refer neurology. Educate carers on neuroleptic danger.
FrontotemporalPersonality change, disinhibition, apathy. Younger onset (50–65). Preserved memory early.Caregiver support, refer neuropsychiatry
1
Non-Pharmacological ManagementCognitive stimulation therapy (CST) ★★★ — equivalent to acetylcholinesterase inhibitors for cognition and QoL. Reality orientation therapy. Reminiscence therapy (photos/music from formative years). Music therapy ★★★ — reduces BPSD. Structured routine — reduces sundowning.
2
Caregiver SupportCaregiver burnout affects 60–70% — screen with PHQ-9 + GAD-7 at every visit. Provide: stage education, practical strategies (wandering, agitation, eating difficulties), respite care advocacy, support groups. Legal planning (power of attorney, advance directive) — discuss early while patient has capacity.

cards.title: Urinary Incontinence — Naturopathic Assessment & Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
Urinary Incontinence — Underreported
UI affects 30–40% of community-dwelling elderly and up to 70% of nursing home residents. Most patients are too embarrassed to report it — always ask directly. It is treatable — not an inevitable part of ageing.
TypeMechanismFeaturesNaturopathic Approach
Stress incontinenceWeak pelvic floor — urethral sphincter incompetenceLeakage with coughing, sneezing, laughing, exercisePelvic floor exercises (Kegel) ★★★★, pessary for women, weight loss, avoid caffeine
Urge incontinence (OAB)Detrusor overactivity — involuntary bladder contractionSudden urge, cannot reach toilet in time, frequency >8x/day, nocturiaBladder training ★★★★, avoid caffeine/alcohol, pumpkin seed extract, magnesium, acupuncture
Mixed (most common in elderly)Both stress + urge componentsBoth leakage typesCombination pelvic floor + bladder training. Address both components
Overflow incontinenceBladder outlet obstruction (BPH men) or underactive detrusorDribbling, incomplete emptying, weak stream, distended bladderRefer — may need catheterisation. Saw palmetto for BPH adjunct
Functional incontinenceCannot reach toilet due to mobility/cognitive issuesNormal bladder but cannot toilet independentlyPrompted voiding, toilet accessibility (grab rails, commode), scheduled toileting
K
Kegel (Pelvic Floor) Exercises Identify correct muscles: stop urinary stream mid-flow (identification only — not regular exercise). Contract pelvic floor, hold 3–5 sec, relax 3–5 sec. Progress to 10 sec hold. 3 sets of 10–15 reps, 3x/day. Results in 6–12 weeks. Biofeedback-assisted pelvic floor training more effective than verbal instruction alone. Refer to pelvic floor physiotherapist for optimal results.
B
Bladder Training (for Urge UI) Scheduled voiding every 2 hours regardless of urge. When urge occurs: STOP, breathe, squeeze pelvic floor × 5, walk calmly to toilet (do not rush — rushing increases urgency). Gradually extend voiding interval by 15 min every week until 3–4 hourly. 6–8 week programme — success rate 75%+. As effective as antimuscarinic medications with no side effects.
D
Dietary Modifications Eliminate: caffeine (irritates detrusor — even decaf), alcohol, artificial sweeteners, carbonated drinks, spicy foods (all bladder irritants). Target 1.5–2L plain water/day — neither restrict (concentrates urine → irritation) nor excess. Pumpkin seed extract 500mg BD — Grade B evidence for urge and stress UI. Magnesium 400mg/day — reduces detrusor overactivity.

cards.title: Palliative & End-of-Life Care — Naturopathic Role
cards._legacyHtml:
Palliative Care — The Naturopathic Philosophy Applies Most Here
Palliative naturopathy focuses on: dignity, comfort, quality of life, and whole-person care. The naturopathic principles of "first do no harm" and "treat the whole person" are never more relevant than at end of life. Goals shift from cure to comfort and meaning.
SymptomNaturopathic InterventionEvidence
PainMassage, TENS, heat, topical capsaicin, acupressure, relaxation techniques. Adjunct to opioids — do not replace★★★ Good as adjuncts
Nausea/vomitingGinger 1g TDS, PC6 acupressure, peppermint aromatherapy, small cold meals★★★ Good
Fatigue (cancer-related)Gentle walking 20–30 min/day (strongest evidence), rest-activity balance, gentle yoga, adaptogens (Ashwagandha — caution in advanced cancer — check oncologist)★★★★ Exercise is gold standard
Anxiety/existential distressMindfulness, guided imagery, music therapy, breathwork, spiritual care, therapeutic presence★★★★ MBSR in cancer — strong
InsomniaSleep hygiene, melatonin 5–10mg, valerian, relaxation, lavender aromatherapy, weighted blanket★★★ Good
Anorexia/cachexiaSmall frequent high-calorie meals, favourite foods (quality over quantity), oral nutritional supplements, mouth care, addressing constipation, ginger for nausea★★★ Good
Constipation (opioid-induced)Senna + docusate (standard), Mg oxide, warm prune juice, abdominal massage, methylnaltrexone (prescriptive — PAMORA)★★★★ Essential — all opioid patients need laxatives
🟡 The Naturopathic Role in Dying
The most profound naturopathic contribution in palliative care is often NOT the supplement or herb — it is: therapeutic presence (being fully present with suffering), facilitating meaning-making (what has mattered in this life?), supporting the family system (caregiver burden is a clinical condition), and ensuring death with dignity. Integrate with the palliative care team — share information, respect the plan, and do not introduce interventions that create false hope or add burden. Vis medicatrix naturae: the innate healing power includes the capacity to die with grace.

cards.title: Sleep in Older Adults
cards._legacyHtml:
Age-Related Changes vs Pathological Sleep
Normal ageing: earlier sleep phase, more fragmented, reduced SWS — but NOT reduced sleep NEED. Total need remains 7–8 hours. "I don't need much sleep" is a common but inaccurate belief in elderly.
Age-Related ChangeNaturopathic Response
Advanced sleep phase syndromeEvening bright light 2500 lux (7–9pm). Delay dinner to 7pm. Gradual sleep delay protocol.
↓ Sleep spindles + SWSExercise ★★★ (increases SWS). Magnesium threonate 200mg (crosses BBB, increases SWS). Bedroom cool 18–20°C.
↓ Melatonin secretionMelatonin 0.5–1mg (NOT 5–10mg) 1–2hrs before sleep. Low dose more physiological in elderly.
Medication-induced insomniaReview: diuretics to morning, beta-blockers to morning. Beers Criteria review. CBT-I before hypnotics — more effective long-term.
1
REM Sleep Behaviour Disorder (RBD)Acting out vivid dreams during REM (punching, kicking, yelling). RBD is a prodromal marker for Parkinson's/Lewy body dementia — 80% develop synucleinopathy within 12 years. Refer neurology for polysomnography. Melatonin 3–12mg at bedtime ★★★ (first-line). Safety: pillow barrier, floor mattress for bed partner.
2
Sundowning ManagementBright light 10,000 lux from 2–4pm ★★★ (resets circadian rhythm, reduces frequency and severity). Melatonin 0.5–1mg at 6pm (BEFORE sundowning occurs — not 10pm). Structured afternoon activity. Minimise daytime napping. Caregiver education: distressing but not dangerous.
